name: Egg Drop Challenge Design Brief
description: Design a protective enclosure for a raw egg that survives a drop from increasing heights. Start by writing a complete design brief: define the problem, identify constraints (materials budget, weight limit, size limit), establish success criteria (egg survives, minimum height), and brainstorm at least 5 different approaches before selecting one. Build, test from 1 meter, analyze results, iterate, and test from 2 meters. Document the full design cycle in an engineering notebook.
learning_objectives: Write a complete design brief with requirements and constraints
Generate multiple solution approaches before selecting one
Use a decision matrix to evaluate options against criteria
Document the design process in an engineering notebook
variations: Foundation (K-2): Drop a toy figure from table height using only paper and tape
High School: Add budget constraints (each material has a cost) and optimize cost-to-performance ratio

name: Customer Needs Interview for a Better Lunchbox
description: Interview 3-5 real people about their frustrations with lunchboxes or lunch containers. Practice empathy as an engineering skill: listen for needs behind complaints, distinguish between stated wants and underlying problems. Translate interview findings into engineering requirements with measurable success criteria. Create a design brief for an improved lunchbox that addresses the top 3 needs identified across all interviews.
learning_objectives: Conduct user research interviews to identify real needs
Distinguish between stated wants and underlying problems
Translate user needs into measurable engineering requirements
Synthesize findings from multiple interviews into a design brief
variations: Elementary: Interview family members about one frustrating object in the house
High School: Add stakeholder analysis covering manufacturer, retailer, and end-user perspectives

name: Strongest Bridge from 100 Popsicle Sticks
description: Design and build a bridge spanning 30 centimeters using exactly 100 popsicle sticks and white glue. The bridge must support weight placed at its center. Start by researching structural shapes (triangles, arches, trusses). Sketch at least 3 different bridge designs. Build your chosen design and test to failure by gradually adding weight. Record the maximum load. Perform failure analysis: where did it break and why? Identify the load path and the weakest point. Write a one-page report on what you would change in version 2.
learning_objectives: Understand how triangulation strengthens structures
Trace load paths through a structure
Test a structure to failure and analyze the failure point
Propose data-driven improvements based on failure analysis
variations: Foundation (K-2): Build the tallest tower from 50 popsicle sticks that stands on its own
High School: Calculate theoretical load capacity using beam equations before building

name: Material Strength Testing Lab
description: Test 5 common materials (paper, cardboard, aluminum foil, balsa wood, fabric) for tension, compression, and bending strength using simple, repeatable tests. For tension: hang weights from strips until they tear. For compression: stack weights on columns until they buckle. For bending: support strips at two points and load the center until failure. Record all data in a materials comparison chart. Rank materials by each property. Discuss tradeoffs: which material is strongest overall? Which is lightest? Which is cheapest? Is there a single best material?
learning_objectives: Measure material properties using controlled, repeatable tests
Organize test data in a structured comparison chart
Understand that materials have different strengths in different modes
Recognize that material selection involves tradeoffs with no single best answer
variations: Foundation (K-2): Sort materials into strong/weak, bendy/stiff, heavy/light categories by feel
High School: Calculate stress and strain values and plot stress-strain curves

name: Build a Simple Machine Chain Reaction
description: Design and build a Rube Goldberg chain reaction machine that uses at least 4 of the 6 simple machines (lever, wheel and axle, pulley, inclined plane, wedge, screw). The machine must complete a simple task such as popping a balloon, ringing a bell, or turning on a flashlight. Each stage triggers the next through energy transfer. The machine must work reliably 3 out of 5 attempts. Document each stage with a sketch showing which simple machine is used and how energy transfers between stages.
learning_objectives: Identify and apply at least 4 of the 6 simple machines
Understand how energy transfers between mechanical stages
Design for reliability through testing and adjustment
Document a system showing component interactions
variations: Foundation (K-2): Build a 3-stage chain reaction using dominoes, a ramp, and a ball
High School: Calculate the mechanical advantage of each stage and the overall system efficiency

name: Design a Gear Train
description: Using physical gears (LEGO Technic, K'NEX, or printed cardboard gears) or a virtual gear simulator, design a gear train that achieves a specified speed change. Challenge 1: reduce input speed by 4x (4:1 reduction). Challenge 2: increase output speed by 3x (1:3 increase). For each challenge, calculate the required gear ratios, predict the torque tradeoff, build the gear train, and test by turning the input and observing the output. Record predicted vs. actual behavior. Explain the speed-torque tradeoff in your own words.
materials: gear set (LEGO Technic, K'NEX, or cardboard gears)
learning_objectives: Calculate gear ratios for specified speed changes
Understand the speed-torque tradeoff in gear systems
Build a working gear train from a calculated design
Compare predicted behavior to actual measured behavior
variations: Elementary: Explore gears freely and observe which combinations make things go faster or slower
High School: Design a multi-stage compound gear train for a specific application like a clock mechanism

name: Paper Airplane Redesign Iteration
description: Fold a standard paper airplane. Fly it 5 times and measure the average distance. Identify one specific thing to change (wing angle, nose weight, wing size, tail shape). Make that single change, refold, and fly 5 more times. Measure the new average. Repeat for 5 iterations, changing only one variable each time. Graph all results showing distance vs. iteration number. Analyze: which change helped the most? Which hurt? What would you try next? This activity teaches that systematic single-variable iteration beats random changes.
learning_objectives: Apply systematic single-variable iteration to improve a design
Collect and graph quantitative test data across multiple iterations
Distinguish between changes that improve performance and those that do not
Understand that iteration is a cycle of test, analyze, and change
variations: Foundation (K-2): Fold, fly, and describe what happened without formal measurement
High School: Add aerodynamic analysis and predict how each change should affect flight

name: Crash Test Dummy Safety Improvement
description: Build a small car from cardboard with an egg as the passenger. Roll the car down a ramp into a wall. Assess egg damage (cracked, broken, intact). Design and build safety features: crumple zones, seat belts (tape), airbags (balloons), roll cage. Add one safety feature at a time, testing after each addition. Record which features helped most. Calculate the improvement percentage after each iteration. Produce a final design that protects the egg from a ramp height of at least 50 centimeters. Write a safety engineering report documenting your iterations.
learning_objectives: Apply iterative design to improve safety features systematically
Test each design change independently to measure its contribution
Perform failure analysis after each crash test
Document engineering iterations with quantitative improvement data
variations: Foundation (K-2): Wrap a toy figure in different materials and drop from table height
High School: Add energy calculations and analyze force distribution through crumple zones

name: Accessibility Audit of a Real Space
description: Audit a real public space (school, park, library, store) for accessibility barriers. Use a wheelchair user checklist (door widths, ramp grades, counter heights, signage), a vision impairment checklist (contrast, tactile markers, audio signals), and a hearing impairment checklist (visual alerts, captions, noise levels). Document each barrier found with a photo or sketch and a proposed engineering solution with rough cost estimate. Prioritize barriers by impact (how many people affected) and feasibility (cost and difficulty to fix). Present your top 3 recommended improvements.
learning_objectives: Identify accessibility barriers in real environments
Propose engineering solutions for accessibility problems
Prioritize improvements using impact and feasibility criteria
Understand that design choices include or exclude people
variations: Elementary: Walk through the school and find 5 things that would be hard to use from a wheelchair
High School: Research ADA compliance standards and audit against specific code requirements

name: Engineering Failure Case Study Analysis
description: Research a famous engineering failure (Tacoma Narrows Bridge, Challenger disaster, Hyatt Regency walkway collapse, Therac-25 radiation machine, or Boeing 737 MAX). Answer four questions: (1) What happened technically? Describe the engineering failure mode. (2) What were the contributing human factors? Describe the organizational, communication, or decision-making failures. (3) What were the consequences for people and society? (4) What changes were made afterward to prevent recurrence? Present findings in a 5-minute oral report or a 2-page written report. Discuss: could this have been prevented, and whose responsibility was it?
materials: computer with internet access for research
presentation materials or paper for written report
learning_objectives: Analyze engineering failures for both technical and human factors
Understand that engineering decisions have real consequences for people
Recognize the role of organizational culture in engineering safety
Articulate ethical responsibilities of engineers to the public
variations: Middle School: Use a pre-selected simplified case study with guided questions
High School: Compare two failures and identify common patterns in engineering ethics breakdowns
